overview: The Marathon County Board held an educational meeting featuring presentations on PFAS contamination litigation opportunities and renewable energy regulation authority. No votes were taken as this was an informational session, but the board received detailed information about joining multi-district litigation against PFAS chemical manufacturers and learned about county options for responding to proposed wind energy projects in the area.
committee: Marathon County Board of Supervisors Educational Meeting
presiding: Not explicitly named in transcript, referred to as 'Mr. Chair'

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Presentation on PFAS multi-district litigation opportunity
Presentation on renewable energy regulatory authority
Discussion of proposed wind projects in Marathon County
Discussion of county ordinances and preemption issues
County options for responding to renewable energy projects

discussions.body: Five residents spoke during public comment. Cindy Nelson from Stratford reported visiting 200 houses and finding no support for wind turbines, stating residents don't want the county board making decisions without providing information. Wendy Rowski from Green Valley urged the board to vote no on the comprehensive plan draft, objecting to the term 'wind farm' and requesting it be called 'industrial wind energy development.' Barb Newton and Cindy Hogan from Rib Mountain spoke in support of reducing speed limits and creating a no-passing zone on Double N, noting 75 residents signed a petition. Heidi Pesky from McMillan presented concerns about joint development agreements, arguing counties don't need JDAs to regulate wind projects and listing potential negative consequences of such agreements.

Attorney Carrie McDougall from Baron and Bud Law Firm presented via WebEx on PFAS litigation opportunities. He explained the firm serves as co-lead counsel in the largest toxic tort settlement in U.S. history, with 3M paying approximately $12-13 billion and DuPont paying $3-5 billion in water contamination cases. McDougall stated Marathon County could file claims related to airport contamination, wastewater, and landfill issues on a 25% contingency fee basis with no upfront costs. Supervisor Robinson asked multiple questions about scope of claims and whether settlements would restrict future recovery. Vice Chair Dickinson noted the airport has no known contamination currently. Supervisor Mash asked about compensation structure, and McDougall confirmed the county would only pay if there is a recovery.

Attorney Rebecca Roker from Atollis Law presented on county authority regarding renewable energy projects, noting the Hub City Wind project from Alliant Energy has no engineering plans filed with PSC yet. She explained PSC has approved 33 solar projects with zero denials, stating 'chances are you're not' going to stop these projects due to state preemption. Roker outlined four options for the county: do nothing, negotiate a joint development agreement (JDA), intervene in PSC proceedings, or litigate. She strongly recommended JDAs as the best tool to protect county interests, noting they can address liability, environmental impacts, road damage, decommissioning, and emergency response training that state law does not otherwise require. She emphasized the county should get involved early before CPCN applications are filed.

publicComment: Five speakers addressed the board: Cindy Nelson (Stratford) opposed wind turbines after surveying 200 residents; Wendy Rowski (Green Valley) objected to 'farm' terminology in the comprehensive plan for industrial energy facilities; Barb Newton (Rib Mountain) supported speed reduction on Double N citing near head-on collisions; Heidi Pesky (McMillan) argued against joint development agreements for wind projects; Cindy Hogan (Rib Mountain) supported the Double N speed reduction petition signed by 75 residents.
actionItems: Board to consider resolution on PFAS litigation engagement at next week's meeting
County to evaluate potential PFAS contamination testing at airport, wastewater, and landfill facilities
Board to consider options for responding to Hub City Wind project before CPCN application is filed
Comprehensive plan draft vote scheduled for next week's meeting
title: Marathon County Board Education Meeting
